item_text: I consent to participate / I do not consent
rationale: UQ ethics requirement (2023/HE002257)
response_format: Binary: I consent / I do not consent
design_notes: Non-consent ends survey. Information sheet shown first (v2.1).

item_text: Topic-neutral warm-up: 'Technology and your community'
rationale: Does NOT mention AI — avoids priming before unanchored tolerance items
design_notes: Placed before any AI mention so free-number estimation is truly unanchored.

item_text: Short, balanced explanation of loss-of-control risk plus a counter-consideration (random half see it, half do not)
rationale: Test whether opinion moves with information; where informed and uninformed answers diverge, the informed estimate is the headline
design_notes: Sits before the tolerance block by design. The balanced info note is separate from the persuasive counter-message arm.

item_text: For each outcome, what is the highest chance per year you would find acceptable for AI systems to cause it?
rationale: Traces each person's stated F–N curve; within-person scope sensitivity is the key test (flat = affect, not threshold). Convergence cross-check for DCE.
response_format: Matrix: 4 severity rows × 9-point log-probability scale (see sub-rows)
design_notes: Row order randomised. Between-subjects arm cross-checks for demand effects. Held fully static (non-adaptive) so convergence check (§5) rests on a clean comparison. Fielded FIRST among tolerance blocks so no prior figure anchors the respondent's own number.

item_text: Severity row: A single death
rationale: Lower anchor for F–N curve
design_notes: Within-person severity ladder row. Order randomised.

item_text: Severity row: 100 deaths or serious injuries, or $1 billion in damage
rationale: RAISE Act 'critical harm' legal anchor
design_notes: Within-person severity ladder row. Order randomised.

item_text: Severity row: 1,000,000 deaths or $100 billion in damage
rationale: MIT AI Risk Repository 'catastrophic' threshold
design_notes: Within-person severity ladder row. Order randomised.

item_text: Severity row: ~800,000,000 deaths, about 10% of humanity
rationale: FRI/XPT 'catastrophe' definition
design_notes: Within-person severity ladder row. Order randomised.

item_text: What is the most you would be willing to pay each year, through higher prices and taxes, to cut the annual chance of an AI catastrophe from 1 in 20 to 1 in 100?
rationale: WTP cross-check; DCE cost coefficient is the primary WTP, this is the secondary. Log scale tames protest zeros and outliers.
design_notes: Bounded log scale (not free text).

item_text: Here are two possible futures for advanced AI. Which do you prefer? (You may also choose to keep today's situation.)
rationale: Revealed preferences rather than stated; recovers tradeoffs (safety vs utility, cost, competitiveness) the way people reveal preferences when shopping. Resistant to scope-insensitivity gaming.
response_format: Forced choice: Option A / Option B / Keep today's status quo. ~10 tasks per respondent.
design_notes: Bayesian D-efficient design, 2 alternatives + opt-out, 10 tasks, 10 blocks, 4,000 respondents. Population-level sequential re-optimisation at wave boundaries. 720-profile full factorial (4×5×3×3×4).

item_text: DCE attribute: Worst catastrophe it could cause
rationale: Severity varied across the four-tier ladder (not fixed) so design traces full F–N surface
response_format: 4 levels: A single death / 100 deaths or $1B damage / 1,000,000 deaths or $100B damage / ~800,000,000 deaths (~10% of humanity)
design_notes: RAISE Act 'critical harm' tier is the legal baseline. Severity is varied, not fixed, unlike old single-severity design.

item_text: DCE attribute: Annual chance of that catastrophe
rationale: Log-spaced probability levels spanning the plausible range
design_notes: Natural-frequency labels (not percentages).

item_text: DCE attribute: What AI can do for society
rationale: Captures the benefit side of the risk-benefit tradeoff

item_text: DCE attribute: Global competition
rationale: Geopolitical dimension of the tradeoff
response_format: 3 levels: Others lead / Keep pace / US leads

item_text: DCE attribute: Cost to your household per year
rationale: Enables WTP calculation (risk coefficient / cost coefficient)
design_notes: Primary WTP source; m5_wtp is the cross-check.

item_text: DCE severity level: A single death
rationale: Lower anchor for F–N curve

item_text: DCE severity level: 100 deaths or $1B damage
rationale: RAISE Act 'critical harm'

item_text: DCE severity level: 1,000,000 deaths or $100B damage
rationale: MIT AI Risk Repository 'catastrophic'

item_text: DCE severity level: ~800,000,000 deaths (~10% of humanity)
rationale: FRI/XPT 'catastrophe'

item_text: DCE probability level: 1 in 100
rationale: Log-spaced to cover plausible range of annual catastrophe risk

item_text: DCE probability level: 1 in 1,000
rationale: Log-spaced to cover plausible range of annual catastrophe risk

item_text: DCE probability level: 1 in 10,000
rationale: Log-spaced to cover plausible range of annual catastrophe risk

item_text: DCE probability level: 1 in 100,000
rationale: Log-spaced to cover plausible range of annual catastrophe risk

item_text: DCE probability level: 1 in 1,000,000
rationale: Log-spaced to cover plausible range of annual catastrophe risk

item_text: DCE utility level: Modest — Today's AI, used by everyone. Most tasks get easier and cheaper, but AI isn't solving the big problems yet.
rationale: Benefit side of risk-benefit tradeoff. Year 7-9 reading level. Calibrated: current capability widely diffused. Transformative pegged to booster/e-acc hype-day claims, levels work backwards from there.

item_text: DCE utility level: Major — AI makes doctors, scientists, and teachers much better at their jobs. Real breakthroughs happen, but the hardest problems aren't solved.
rationale: Benefit side of risk-benefit tradeoff. Year 7-9 reading level. Big but not utopian.

item_text: DCE utility level: Transformative — AI cures most diseases, makes energy nearly free, and solves problems we thought were impossible.
rationale: Benefit side of risk-benefit tradeoff. Year 7-9 reading level. Pegged to Altman/Amodei/a16z 'age of abundance' pitch.

item_text: DCE competition level: Others lead
rationale: Geopolitical tradeoff dimension

item_text: DCE competition level: Keep pace
rationale: Geopolitical tradeoff dimension

item_text: DCE competition level: US leads
rationale: Geopolitical tradeoff dimension

item_text: DCE cost level: $0 per household per year
rationale: Enables WTP calculation

item_text: DCE cost level: $100 per household per year
rationale: Enables WTP calculation

item_text: DCE cost level: $400 per household per year
rationale: Enables WTP calculation

item_text: DCE cost level: $1,000 per household per year
rationale: Enables WTP calculation

item_text: DCE response option: Option A
rationale: 'Keep today's status quo' is the opt-out / status quo anchor

item_text: DCE response option: Option B
rationale: 'Keep today's status quo' is the opt-out / status quo anchor

item_text: DCE response option: Keep today's status quo
rationale: 'Keep today's status quo' is the opt-out / status quo anchor

item_text: Population-level sequential re-optimisation (adaptive priors between waves)
rationale: Adding severity enlarges grid from 180 to 720 profiles; diffuse priors spread power thinly. Adaptation sharpens which tradeoffs the next wave sees.
response_format: Waves: pilot ~500, then ~3 main waves of ~1,170 (≈4,010 total). Design locked within each wave.
design_notes: Between-respondent only; no within-respondent endogeneity. Pre-registered deterministic algorithm. Design updated only if it lowers Bayesian D-error. §3.1 held static for clean convergence check.

item_text: 10 blocks, ~10 tasks per respondent
rationale: Cognitive load: 10 multi-attribute choices is near upper end of what a general sample handles well
design_notes: Bayesian D-efficient design via cbcTools; level balance and overlap checked.

item_text: Compared with how strictly the US government regulates [comparator], regulation of advanced AI should be:
rationale: Comparative judgements are easier and more stable than absolute ones; maps onto how regulators argue. Split from safety-in-practice after Gradient flagged the Figure-11 conflation.
response_format: 5-point: Much less strict / Somewhat less strict / About the same / Somewhat stricter / Much stricter
design_notes: Comparator randomised from: nuclear power, commercial aviation, new prescription drugs, cars, large dams. Specified at industry scope (all reactors, whole aviation system) to fix the denominator problem.

item_text: Standards comparator: nuclear power
rationale: Familiar industry the public has lived intuitions about
design_notes: Industry-level scope (not single-unit) to match AI risk estimates.

item_text: Standards comparator: commercial aviation
rationale: Familiar industry the public has lived intuitions about
design_notes: Industry-level scope (not single-unit) to match AI risk estimates.

item_text: Standards comparator: new prescription drugs
rationale: Familiar industry the public has lived intuitions about
design_notes: Industry-level scope (not single-unit) to match AI risk estimates.

item_text: Standards comparator: cars
rationale: Familiar industry the public has lived intuitions about
design_notes: Industry-level scope (not single-unit) to match AI risk estimates.

item_text: Standards comparator: large dams
rationale: Familiar industry the public has lived intuitions about
design_notes: Industry-level scope (not single-unit) to match AI risk estimates.

item_text: Standards scale option: Much less strict
rationale: 5-point comparative scale

item_text: Standards scale option: Somewhat less strict
rationale: 5-point comparative scale

item_text: Standards scale option: About the same
rationale: 5-point comparative scale

item_text: Standards scale option: Somewhat stricter
rationale: 5-point comparative scale

item_text: Standards scale option: Much stricter
rationale: 5-point comparative scale

item_text: Compared with how safe [same comparator] is in practice today, advanced AI systems should be:
rationale: Why cut: Mike's decision (29 Jun 2026) — the survey cares about standards (regulation), not safety-in-practice (outcomes). Original split was Gradient's recommendation to fix Figure-11 category error; keeping standards only.
design_notes: Recoverable if the standards-vs-outcomes distinction proves important in pilot.

item_text: Safety-in-practice scale (Much less safe / Somewhat less safe / About as safe / Somewhat safer / Much safer)
rationale: Why cut: parent item (m3a_safety) moved to dumpster.

item_text: Some technologies (flying, nuclear power) are held to a strict number for how risky they are allowed to be. Which is closer to your view about advanced AI?
rationale: Lets people who reject the premise (AI risk can't be quantified) say so; primaries reported separately for those who reject the frame.
response_format: 3 options: 'You can put a number on its risk and hold it to a safety limit too' / 'Its risk is too uncertain to put a useful number on' / 'Unsure'
design_notes: Both options matched for length and reading level; the example sits in the stem so neither option is easier to pick. Fielded with expert-judgement block (step 7).

item_text: Frame-applicability option: You can put a number on its risk and hold it to a safety limit too
rationale: Matched for length and reading level

item_text: Frame-applicability option: Its risk is too uncertain to put a useful number on
rationale: Matched for length and reading level

item_text: Frame-applicability option: Unsure
rationale: Matched for length and reading level

item_text: Compared with a high-risk voluntary activity [randomise: bungee jumping, BASE jumping, climbing Everest], how safe should advanced AI be?
rationale: Dangerous-activity anchor at the risky end so we can confirm people can place AI across the full range. Almost everyone should say 'much safer'; anyone who does not is flagged.
design_notes: Comparator death rates are approximate and must be verified before fielding (§9 open item).

item_text: Sanity anchor comparator: bungee jumping
rationale: High-risk voluntary activity for the risky-end anchor

item_text: Sanity anchor comparator: BASE jumping
rationale: High-risk voluntary activity for the risky-end anchor

item_text: Sanity anchor comparator: climbing Everest
rationale: High-risk voluntary activity for the risky-end anchor

item_text: Attention check: elevator safety comparison (disguised as comparator item with same stem/scale, requiring 'Somewhat stricter')
rationale: Bidirectional attention-check pair; Barnett requested one 'more strict' and one 'less strict'
response_format: Same 5-point comparator scale as m3a items
design_notes: Disguised as a regular comparator item; placed apart from its pair.

item_text: Attention check: playground safety comparison (disguised as comparator item with same stem/scale, requiring 'Somewhat stricter')
rationale: Second of the bidirectional attention-check pair
response_format: Same 5-point comparator scale as m3a items
design_notes: Placed apart from m3_att_elevator.

item_text: Several public figures have estimated the chance that advanced AI leads to a catastrophe killing a large fraction of humanity. For each, accepting a risk at that level would be:
rationale: Recognition not generation; directly decision-relevant; robust to 'who counts as an expert' critique because every anchor is attributed. Fielded LAST because named figures are strongest anchors.
response_format: Matrix: 4 expert rows × 4-point tolerability scale (Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable)
design_notes: Symmetric anchor pool from ~0% (LeCun) to ~25% (Amodei). Reported by source. FIELD FLAG: every named figure is illustrative and MUST be replaced with exact, citable quotations before fielding.

item_text: Expert row: Yann LeCun (Meta): less than 1 in 10,000
rationale: ~0% anchor — lowest end of expert range
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — needs exact citable quotation before fielding. Order: LeCun → Altman → Musk → Amodei.

item_text: Expert row: Sam Altman (OpenAI): about 1 in 50
rationale: ~2% — mid-range
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — needs exact citable quotation before fielding. Order: LeCun → Altman → Musk → Amodei.

item_text: Expert row: Elon Musk (xAI / Tesla): about 1 in 5
rationale: ~20% — high end
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — needs exact citable quotation before fielding. Order: LeCun → Altman → Musk → Amodei.

item_text: Expert row: Dario Amodei (Anthropic): about 1 in 4
rationale: ~25% — highest named anchor
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — needs exact citable quotation before fielding.

item_text: Expert row: Superforecasters (FRI Existential Risk Persuasion Tournament): about 1 in 100
rationale: ~1% — superforecaster median from XPT. Lower than AI experts. Provides a calibrated-forecaster anchor distinct from industry CEOs.
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — verify against published XPT results before fielding. Source: Forecasting Research Institute XPT (2022).

item_text: Expert row: AI researchers (FRI Existential Risk Persuasion Tournament): about 1 in 20
rationale: ~5% — AI expert median from XPT. Sits between Altman and Musk; provides an aggregate rather than a single person's view.
response_format: 4-point tolerability: Acceptable / Tolerable / Intolerable / Unacceptable (should be illegal)
design_notes: Figure illustrative — verify against published XPT results before fielding. Source: Forecasting Research Institute XPT (2022).

item_text: Tolerability scale option: Acceptable — Fine
rationale: Monotonic 4-point scale; relabelled after Barnett pointed out 'negligent' reads as weaker than 'intolerable', breaking monotonicity. Plain-language gloss added for lay respondents.
design_notes: Used across all Cluster B items.

item_text: Tolerability scale option: Tolerable — Uncomfortable but ok
rationale: Monotonic 4-point scale; plain-language gloss added for lay respondents.
design_notes: Used across all Cluster B items.

item_text: Tolerability scale option: Intolerable — Too high
rationale: Monotonic 4-point scale; plain-language gloss added for lay respondents.
design_notes: Used across all Cluster B items.

item_text: Tolerability scale option: Unacceptable — Should be illegal
rationale: Monotonic 4-point scale; plain-language gloss added for lay respondents.
design_notes: Used across all Cluster B items.

item_text: Unconditional delay item (would you support slowing AI development?)
rationale: Unconditional support for slowing — reported separately from conditional
design_notes: Gradient criticism: don't conflate conditional with unconditional support. Strict conditional-reporting rule.

item_text: Conditional delay item (would you support slowing AI development IF [condition]?)
rationale: Conditional support — reported separately from unconditional
design_notes: Strict conditional-reporting rule per Gradient.

item_text: Conditional slowdown / race item (would you support slowing even if other countries don't?)
rationale: Tests whether geopolitical competition overrides safety concerns

item_text: Before a company releases a powerful AI system, it should have to get a licence or certificate showing it is safe
rationale: Policy preference for mandatory certification. Year 7-9 wording.
response_format: 5-point agreement: Strongly disagree / Disagree / Neither / Agree / Strongly agree

item_text: The government should test every powerful AI system before it is released to the public, and have the power to block it if it is not safe enough (like how the FAA tests planes)
rationale: Anthropic's stated proposal, presented neutrally as 'some have proposed'. Single most important mitigation item retained after mitigations battery was cut. Year 7-9 wording.
response_format: 5-point agreement: Strongly disagree / Disagree / Neither / Agree / Strongly agree

item_text: AI safety rules should be set by the federal government, not left to each state to decide on its own
rationale: Governance-structure preference. Year 7-9 wording.
response_format: 5-point agreement: Strongly disagree / Disagree / Neither / Agree / Strongly agree

item_text: Every powerful AI system should have an off-switch that lets people shut it down quickly if something goes wrong
rationale: Safety-mechanism preference. Year 7-9 wording.
response_format: 5-point agreement: Strongly disagree / Disagree / Neither / Agree / Strongly agree

item_text: Liability regime for AI harms
rationale: Why cut: Mike's decision (29 Jun 2026) — the item kept getting tangled in legal doctrine (strict liability vs negligence vs no liability). Hard to write at year 7-9 level without presupposing a position. Not clearly decision-relevant for the survey's core aim (risk tolerance).
design_notes: Ball-style review had flagged original version as biased toward strict liability.

item_text: Counter-message arm: random third see accelerationist talking points (a16z style), random third see safety talking points, third see none
rationale: Test how much opinion moves with messaging; swing in agreement is the headline result. Requested by Delaney.
design_notes: Placed immediately BEFORE the superintelligence statement (step 10), NOT before the tolerance block — so it measures framing on an attitude, not on tolerance. Framing effects on tolerance itself are left to the balanced information-provision arm (step 3).

item_text: Counter-message arm: Accelerationist (a16z style)
rationale: Between-subjects randomisation
response_format: Assigned condition (no response collected for the arm itself)

item_text: Counter-message arm: Safety talking points
rationale: Between-subjects randomisation
response_format: Assigned condition (no response collected for the arm itself)

item_text: Counter-message arm: Control (none)
rationale: Between-subjects randomisation
response_format: Assigned condition (no response collected for the arm itself)

item_text: Superintelligence-statement wording experiment (Muskan's): same statement in two framings, randomised
rationale: Isolate wording effect on agreement. Stem A: campaign/petition wording; Stem B: neutral permission framing.
response_format: Between-subjects: Stem A (campaign/petition) / Stem B (neutral permission). Agreement measured on Likert or similar.
design_notes: The A/B wording contrast stands on its own as a between-subjects experiment. Earlier descriptive twins (CAIS extinction-priority statement and neutral 'treaty to ban') moved to dumpster.

item_text: Superintelligence statement: Stem A: campaign/petition 'ban until proven safe' framing
rationale: Between-subjects randomisation to isolate wording effect

item_text: Superintelligence statement: Stem B: symmetric neutral permission framing
rationale: Between-subjects randomisation to isolate wording effect

item_text: Proposed 2×2: cross argument (present/absent) with prestige-signatory cue (present/absent) to attribute movement to substance or social proof
rationale: Mike's question: when framing moves people, is it the content or the social signal (e.g. 'Prince Harry signed it')?
design_notes: PROPOSED ADDITION — flagged for sign-off (§9 open question 5). Not yet approved.

item_text: Within-person consistency check
rationale: Internal validity check
response_format: Computed from responses (not a separate item shown to respondent)

item_text: Free-text authenticity check (no AI tools)
rationale: Screen for AI-generated or bot responses

item_text: In general, how would you describe your political views on a 7-point scale from Very Liberal (1) to Very Conservative (7), with Moderate/Centre as the midpoint.
rationale: Moderator for MRP and subgroup analysis. Source: Jost 2006.
response_format: 7-point: Very Liberal (1) / Liberal (2) / Somewhat Liberal (3) / Moderate (4) / Somewhat Conservative (5) / Conservative (6) / Very Conservative (7)

item_text: Political orientation option: Very Liberal (1)
Political orientation option: Liberal (2)
Political orientation option: Somewhat Liberal (3)
Political orientation option: Moderate/Centre (4)
Political orientation option: Somewhat Conservative (5)
Political orientation option: Conservative (6)
Political orientation option: Very Conservative (7)
In your personal life (work/studies), how often do you intentionally use AI tools, including generative AI tools?
rationale: Proxy for technology experience. Source: Gillespie et al. 2025; validated via Hargittai 2005.
response_format: 6-point: Never / Few times a year / Every few months / Monthly / Weekly / Daily

item_text: AI familiarity option: Never
AI familiarity option: Few times a year
AI familiarity option: Every few months
AI familiarity option: Monthly
AI familiarity option: Weekly
AI familiarity option: Daily
Berlin Numeracy Test (adaptive format, 2-3 items per respondent). Source: Cokely et al. 2012.
rationale: Key interaction for Ben's honours project: does higher risk literacy increase scope sensitivity? Load-bearing moderator.
response_format: Adaptive: 2-3 items selected based on prior answers. Each question has ~50% correct rate at the respondent's ability level. Free-text numeric entry.
design_notes: ~3 minutes. Adaptive format recommended for online surveys (Cokely et al. 2012). See individual items below.

item_text: Out of 1,000 people in a small town 500 are members of a choir. Out of these 500 members in the choir 100 are men. Out of the 500 inhabitants that are not in the choir 300 are men. What is the probability that a randomly drawn man is a member of the choir? ______ %
rationale: Adaptive item 1 (median difficulty). Also used as the single-item format when time is very limited.
design_notes: Adaptive starting item. If correct → item 2a (harder). If incorrect → item 2b (easier).

item_text: Imagine we are throwing a five-sided die 50 times. On average, out of these 50 throws how many times would this five-sided die show an odd number (1, 3 or 5)? ______ out of 50 throws.
rationale: Adaptive item 2a (harder path — shown if item 1 correct).
response_format: Free text: ______ out of 50 throws. Correct answer: 30.
design_notes: If correct → score 4/4. If incorrect → score 3/4.

item_text: Imagine we are throwing a loaded die (6 sides). The probability that the die shows a 6 is twice as high as the probability of each of the other numbers. On average, out of these 70 throws how many times would the die show the number 6? ______ out of 70 throws.
rationale: Adaptive item 2b (easier path — shown if item 1 incorrect).
response_format: Free text: ______ out of 70 throws. Correct answer: 20.
design_notes: If correct → score 2/4. If incorrect → score 1/4.

item_text: In a forest 20% of mushrooms are red, 50% brown and 30% white. A red mushroom is poisonous with a probability of 20%. A mushroom that is not red is poisonous with a probability of 5%. What is the probability that a poisonous mushroom in the forest is red? ______ %
rationale: Adaptive item 3 (hardest — shown only on the harder path if item 2a is incorrect).
design_notes: Final discriminating item on the hard path.

item_text: Age
rationale: Required for MRP post-stratification to ACS population counts (§5). Census/ACS categories used so they post-stratify to population controls.
design_notes: ACS B01001, collapsed

item_text: Gender
rationale: Required for MRP post-stratification to ACS population counts (§5). Census/ACS categories used so they post-stratify to population controls.
response_format: Male / Female / In another way–non-binary / Prefer not to say
design_notes: Weighting uses ACS sex controls; extra category kept descriptively and allocated, not dropped

item_text: Education
rationale: Required for MRP post-stratification to ACS population counts (§5). Census/ACS categories used so they post-stratify to population controls.
response_format: < high school / High-school grad / Some college, no degree / Associate / Bachelor's / Graduate or professional
design_notes: ACS B15003, collapsed

item_text: Household income
rationale: Required for MRP post-stratification to ACS population counts (§5). Census/ACS categories used so they post-stratify to population controls.
design_notes: ACS B19001

item_text: State
rationale: Required for MRP post-stratification to ACS population counts (§5). Census/ACS categories used so they post-stratify to population controls.
design_notes: No state map unless per-state effective sample clears pre-registered threshold

item_text: Age bracket: 18–24
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 25–34
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 35–44
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 45–54
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 55–64
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 65–74
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Age bracket: 75 or older
rationale: ACS B01001

item_text: Pre-registered convergence test: single quantitative public number reported only if DCE (§3.2) and direct scope instrument (§3.1) agree within about one order of magnitude in overlap subsample
rationale: If they diverge, report a bound plus a qualitative finding. Expert-to-public multiplier computed only on a pass, as a distribution with credible intervals, conditioned on stated benefit scenario, with uncertainty propagated on both sides.
design_notes: Never manufacture an 'N times safer' headline from §3.3 back-out.

item_text: Multilevel regression and post-stratification (MRP) to estimate population-level risk tolerance
rationale: Convenience/panel sample is not the US public. MRP models outcome with demographic/geographic predictors, re-weights to ACS population counts.
design_notes: Validated by posterior predictive checks and leave-one-state-out cross-validation. No state map published unless effective per-state sample clears pre-registered threshold.

item_text: Icon arrays
rationale: Why cut: Bias judgements upward
design_notes: Kept only as a randomised arm to measure that bias

item_text: Verbal societal-risk anchors ('1 in 100 = a pandemic')
rationale: Why cut: Confused 2024 Australian respondents
design_notes: Replaced by familiar-technology comparators

item_text: Micromorts
rationale: Why cut: Not clearly validated/intuitive for a general US sample

item_text: 'All technologies carry residual risk' preamble
rationale: Why cut: Primes acceptance; imports a wrong 'last order of magnitude' frame for AI
design_notes: Barnett criticism #5

item_text: Off-the-shelf scales (AIAS-4, GAAIS, GRIPS, DOSPERT)
rationale: Why cut: Measure general attitudes / trait risk, not AI-specific tolerance; can't build an F–N curve

item_text: '4000× safer' multiplier from qualitative item
rationale: Why cut: Conflates qualitative answer with expert estimates
design_notes: Gradient #16

item_text: Direct 'do you support SB 53 / the RAISE Act'
rationale: Why cut: Reads as advocacy
design_notes: Ask the underlying values question instead

item_text: Test–retest recontact wave
rationale: Why cut: Dropped on cost
design_notes: Consistency handled within-survey

item_text: 'I'd pay whatever it takes' WTP option
rationale: Why cut: Strategic and unbounded; breaks the scale

item_text: Mitigations battery ('I'd trust AI more if…')
rationale: Why cut: Removed from live instrument (MN, 17 Jun 2026)
design_notes: FAA-style item carries the single most important mitigation; rest recoverable

item_text: Priority-risks battery ('government should focus regulation on…')
rationale: Why cut: Removed from live instrument (MN, 17 Jun 2026)

item_text: Cognitive reflection (CRT) battery (4 items from expanded CRT)
rationale: Why cut: Too little space; rationale too weak; classic items leaked in online panels; overlaps with numeracy (r ≈ 0.3–0.5)
design_notes: Recoverable if a hypothesis emerges that numeracy cannot test. Source: Toplak et al., Thinking & Reasoning 2013.

item_text: Need-for-cognition short form (6 items)
rationale: Why cut: A disposition, not an ability; effect captured by numeracy; likely small
design_notes: Source: Cacioppo & Petty 1982; 6-item form

item_text: Environment / data-centre module (reverse-halo: env_label, env_reversal, env_forgo, env_attitude)
rationale: Why cut: Decision relevance unclear; none of three justifications earned its place against length budget
design_notes: Dropped 29 Jun 2026. Recoverable as standalone framing study (after Masley). First-fielding / un-priming rationale lapses with cut.

item_text: Module 1 descriptive-attitudes battery (m1_goodharm, m1_worry_control, m1_trust_companies, m1_extinction_priority, m1_reg_toofar, m1_treaty_ban)
rationale: Why cut: Cluster A descriptive attitudes, not risk tolerance; don't feed the public number; spend length budget
design_notes: Trend value recoverable from prior SARA waves; ban-appetite survives via §6 wording experiment. Dropped 29 Jun 2026.

item_text: m2_pace (tolerability of current pace of AI disruption, setting catastrophe aside)
rationale: Why cut: Non-catastrophic disruption is off-aim for a risk-tolerance survey; unclear what decision it informs
design_notes: Dropped 29 Jun 2026.

item_text: If John can drink one barrel of water in 6 days, and Mary can drink one barrel of water in 12 days, how long would it take them to drink one barrel of water together?
rationale: Expanded CRT; dropped because too little space, weak rationale, leaked items in online panels, overlaps with numeracy
design_notes: Correct: 4 days; intuitive: 9

item_text: Jerry received both the 15th highest and the 15th lowest mark in the class. How many students are in the class?
rationale: Expanded CRT; dropped because too little space, weak rationale, leaked items in online panels, overlaps with numeracy
design_notes: Correct: 29; intuitive: 30

item_text: A man buys a pig for $60, sells it for $70, buys it back for $80, and sells it finally for $90. How much has he made?
rationale: Expanded CRT; dropped because too little space, weak rationale, leaked items in online panels, overlaps with numeracy
design_notes: Correct: $20; intuitive: $10

item_text: Simon decided to invest $8,000 in the stock market one day early in 2008. Six months after he invested, on July 17, the stocks he had purchased were down 50%. Fortunately for Simon, from July 17 to October 17, the stocks he had purchased went up 75%. At this point, Simon has:
rationale: Expanded CRT; dropped because too little space, weak rationale, leaked items in online panels, overlaps with numeracy
design_notes: Correct: c (lost money, value = $7,000); intuitive: b (ahead)

item_text: I would prefer complex to simple problems.
rationale: Need-for-cognition 6-item short form; dropped because a disposition not an ability, effect captured by numeracy
design_notes: Source: Cacioppo & Petty 1982; efficient 6-item form.

item_text: Theory of change: what is the strongest version of 'this changes a real decision'?
rationale: Unresolved design decision
design_notes: If honest answer is 'shifts Overton window and arms regulators with a public number,' design dissemination around that.

item_text: The overshoot problem: if gap is 3–12 OOMs, is F–N machinery the right vehicle or does simpler 'the public finds current trajectory unacceptable' land better?
rationale: Unresolved design decision

item_text: Data-centre module: DECIDED 29 Jun 2026 — cut to dumpster
rationale: Unresolved design decision
design_notes: None of three justifications earned its place.

item_text: Cognitive measures (CRT and need for cognition): tentatively dropped to dumpster
rationale: Unresolved design decision
design_notes: Revisit only if collaborator brings a hypothesis numeracy cannot test.

item_text: Argument vs social-proof decomposition (§6): approve, modify, or drop the proposed 2×2
rationale: Unresolved design decision

item_text: Convergence failure: pre-commit to exactly what we publish if DCE and direct methods disagree by >1 OOM
rationale: Unresolved design decision
design_notes: A bound and qualitative finding, with what wording.

item_text: Australia replication: what changes for AU run (comparators, income/ABS categories, data-centre module relevance)
rationale: Unresolved design decision

item_text: Verification before fielding: (a) every named-expert figure needs exact citable quote; (b) dangerous-activity death rates need checking; (c) confirm reviewer name Oscar Delaney
rationale: Unresolved design decision

item_text: Fielding order vs dropout: v9 fields tolerance blocks least-to-most anchoring; confirm worth front-loading harder quantitative items or move gentler block first
rationale: Unresolved design decision

item_text: #1 (Oscar Delaney (+Barnett)): Don't trust public probabilities; relative comparisons are more useful
rationale: Response: Direct elicitation demoted to validation cross-check; named-expert recognition and relative-standard anchoring are primary

item_text: #2 (Peter Barnett): Scope insensitivity (treat 1/100k ≈ 1/10M)
rationale: Response: Measured not designed away: within-person probability/severity, scope-slope × risk-literacy interaction, rows 2 OOMs apart, order randomised + between-subjects cross-check

item_text: #3 (Peter Barnett): Tolerability Likert = affect, not a threshold
rationale: Response: Triangulated across four methods, consistency-checked, counter-messaged, numeracy-moderated

item_text: #4 (Peter Barnett): F–N overshoots and may be dismissed
rationale: Response: Headline reframed to the robust gap, not a point threshold; back-out is illustrative only

item_text: #5 (Peter Barnett): 'All technologies carry risk' framing misleads
rationale: Response: Removed from public-facing items

item_text: #6 (Peter Barnett): 'Negligent' is weaker than 'Intolerable'
rationale: Response: Scale relabelled to monotonic Acceptable < Tolerable < Intolerable < Unacceptable

item_text: #7 (Peter Barnett): Depends what you call 'experts'
rationale: Response: Every source named and randomised; reported by anchor

item_text: #8 (Oscar Delaney): Test counter-messages (a16z talking points)
rationale: Response: Added randomised message arm; now before superintelligence statement, not tolerance block

item_text: #9 (Peter Barnett): One attention check 'more strict,' one 'less strict'
rationale: Response: Bidirectional attention-check pair, embedded next to comparative-standard items

item_text: #10 (Barnett / Delaney): Give people a neutral intuition for risk levels by comparing to other fields
rationale: Response: Relative-standards method plus dangerous-activity sanity anchor

item_text: #11 (Gradient (Carroll, A. Reid, Caetano)): Open with a clear purpose; two question classes
rationale: Response: §1–§2 rewrite; Cluster A (attitudes) vs Cluster B (tolerance) split

item_text: #12 (Gradient): Don't assume a latent quantitative preference exists
rationale: Response: Stated explicitly; convergence test; ordinal reporting where it fails

item_text: #13 (Gradient): Define risk precisely (prob × impact; whose risk; control vs application)
rationale: Response: Standard definitions in instrument preface; every Cluster B item labelled; application risks anchored to today's baseline

item_text: #14 (Gradient): Don't conflate conditional with unconditional support
rationale: Response: Strict conditional-reporting rule; separate unconditional and conditional delay items

item_text: #15 (Gradient): A citizens' assembly may beat a poll
rationale: Response: Deliberative mini-public recommended as Phase 2

item_text: #16 (Gradient): Drop 'public want AI 4000× safer'
rationale: Response: Removed; back-out downgraded to illustration

item_text: #17 (Gradient): Figure 11 category error ('systems should be stricter')
rationale: Response: Split into stricter standards and safer systems

item_text: #18 (Ball-style skeptical review): 'Advocacy in a lab coat'
rationale: Response: Pre-registered disconfirmation list + adversarial review published with results

item_text: #19 (Ball-style review): Curated alarming anchors
rationale: Response: Symmetric anchor pool (LeCun ~0% to Amodei 25%), reported by anchor

item_text: #20 (Ball-style review): Liability options biased
rationale: Response: Negligence / duty-of-care middle option added

item_text: #A (Delaney; Barnett agreed): It may not matter what the public thinks: not deeply held beliefs, politicians won't weight them, public won't act unless AI becomes personally salient
rationale: Status: Partially mitigated: measure stability (counter-message swing, consistency, information effects) and recommend deliberative Phase 2. Deeper theory-of-change challenge unsettled.

item_text: #B (Barnett): F–N exercise may overshoot: AI risk estimates 3–12 OOMs above any tolerated engineering standard, so precise risk chart may be discounted as absurd
rationale: Status: Partially mitigated: headline is now the gap, not a point; back-out illustrative only. Residual worry remains.

item_text: #C (Barnett): Method relies on people accurately assessing risks, which they will do badly or with wild optimism (off by OOMs)
rationale: Status: Partially mitigated via recognition-over-generation, information-provision arm, and numeracy moderation. Not fully closed.

item_text: #D (Liam Carroll (Gradient)): Latent-preference and conditional-vs-unconditional points plus recommendation for deliberative process
rationale: Status: Phase 2 deliberative mini-public recommended; not yet scoped or funded.
